title: Pork Vindaloo
description: Pork Vindaloo is one of Goa's most iconic dishes, known for its bold flavors, tangy vinegar marinade, and aromatic spices. Slow-cooked until the pork is tender, this classic curry delivers a perfect balance of heat, acidity, and richness.
time: Prep time: 20 min (plus marination) | Cook time: 1 hr 15 min

ingredients.title: For the Marinade
items: 1 kg (2.2 lb) pork shoulder or pork belly, cut into 2.5–3 cm (1–1.25") cubes
3–4 fresh red chilies (adjust heat to taste)
3 tbsp white wine vinegar (or malt vinegar for tang)
1–2 tsp Kashmiri red chili powder (or mix with cayenne for more heat)

ingredients.title: For the Curry
items: 2 fresh tomatoes, finely chopped (or 200 g canned chopped tomatoes)
400 ml (1⅔ cups) water or light pork stock
1 tbsp jaggery (or brown sugar), optional — balances acidity
Fresh coriander leaves, chopped, for garnish

instruction.title: Preparation
steps: Blend garlic, ginger, red chilies, vinegar, and all dry spices into a smooth paste. Add a splash of water if needed.
Rub the paste thoroughly into the pork cubes.
Cover and refrigerate at least 4 hours, preferably overnight for depth of flavor.

instruction.title: Build the Base
steps: Heat ghee/oil in a heavy-bottomed pan over medium heat.
Add onions and cook slowly, stirring often, until deep golden brown (12–15 min) — this sweetness balances the spice.
Stir in chopped tomatoes and cook until the oil begins to separate from the masala (6–8 min).

instruction.title: Cook the Pork
steps: Add the marinated pork (with all marinade) to the pan. Sear on all sides for 5–6 minutes.
Add green chilies (if using), then pour in water or stock.
Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook gently over low heat for 50–60 minutes, stirring occasionally. The pork should be tender and the sauce thick.
Stir in jaggery (if using) and garam masala in the last 5 minutes. Adjust salt and acidity to taste.

servingSuggestions.title: Serving Suggestions
items: Pair with traditional Goan poee bread.

chefTips: Meat cut: Pork shoulder gives the right balance of tenderness and fat for a silky sauce; pork belly adds richness but can be heavy — use a mix for balance.
Vinegar choice: White wine vinegar is cleaner; malt vinegar gives a more traditional tang.
Oil separation: In professional kitchens, the sign of a finished vindaloo is when oil visibly floats on top — don’t rush this stage.
Make-ahead: Vindaloo tastes better the next day after flavors meld — reheat gently.
